module: Immunocompromised patients Part II: Mechanisms of Acquired Immunodeficiency

sections.How the normal immune function works: Three layers: Physical barriers ~99% (skin, cilia, stomach acidity, normal flora); Innate immune responses ~0.9% (neutrophils, macrophages, NK cells, complement, mannose-binding lectin, acute phase proteins — rapid, non-specific, no memory); Adaptive immune responses ~0.1% (B-lymphocytes: humoral/antibody; T-lymphocytes: cell-mediated — specific, memory). PRRs recognise PAMPs and DAMPs, activating NF-kB and AP-1. MBL deficiency increases risk from encapsulated organisms. Adaptive: B-cells opsonise encapsulated bacteria; T-cells (CD4+/CD8+) clear intracellular pathogens. IL-6 has both pro- and anti-inflammatory effects.
sections.Non Pharmacological Mechanisms: TBI: myeloablative conditioning for HSCT; profound pancytopenia; most common infections: coagulase-negative Staphylococcus (48%) and CMV reactivation (30%). Plasmapheresis: removes up to 60% of serum immunoglobulins and complement per session; encapsulated organism risk; IVIg if IgG <500 mg/dL; inadvertently removes IV antibiotics — re-dose post-procedure. TPN: impairs NF-kB signalling (no enteral stimulation); decreased CD4+ in Peyer's patches; infections: Staphylococcus, Candida, Acinetobacter, Pseudomonas; hyperglycaemia and long-term IV access are additional risks. Pregnancy: Th1-to-Th2 shift; cytotoxic T-cell response suppressed; risks include Listeria, GBS, Toxoplasma, viral infections. Splenectomy: encapsulated organisms (N. meningitidis, S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae); lost IgM memory B-cell opsonisation; vaccinate before elective splenectomy; lifelong increased risk.
sections.Glucocorticoids: Mechanisms: (1) direct gene expression via GCR; (2) indirect NF-kB/AP-1 inhibition; (3) second-messenger effects. Acute effects: neutrophilia (demargination — not increased marrow production) + lymphopenia (T-cells > B-cells suppressed; NK cells unaffected). Impaired neutrophil chemotaxis despite neutrophilia (reduced IL-8). Apoptosis of dendritic cells; impaired antigen presentation. PCP prophylaxis: required for patients on ≥20 mg prednisolone equivalent daily for >30 days. GC in septic shock: suggested if vasopressor ≥0.25 μg/kg/min catecholamine equivalent for ≥6h.
sections.Chemotherapy immunotherapy and biologic: Traditional chemotherapy: non-specific bone marrow toxicity; neutropenia is primary infection predictor; ~80% of neutropenic fever from endogenous flora. Key biologics: Rituximab (anti-CD20): B-cell depletion, hypogammaglobulinaemia, HBV reactivation, PCP, PML. Alemtuzumab (anti-CD52): depletes mature T and B cells; CD4+ depletion analogous to HIV — CMV, HSV, VZV, PCP, TB. Anti-TNF-α (infliximab, adalimumab): impairs granuloma formation; reactivation TB, Histoplasma, Cryptococcus, Coccidioides; HBV reactivation; screen ALL patients for latent TB before starting. Checkpoint inhibitors (PD-1/PD-L1/CTLA-4): 'Domino effect' — no intrinsic infection risk; irAEs require steroids/immunosuppression → PCP, IFI, TB. Eculizumab (anti-C5): blocks MAC → Neisseria risk; vaccinate. mTOR inhibitors: impaired innate immunity, reduced neutrophil migration.
sections.Immunosuppression for solid organ transp: Standard triple therapy: glucocorticoid + calcineurin inhibitor (cyclosporin/tacrolimus: prevent IL-2 transcription) + antiproliferative (mycophenolate/azathioprine). mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus): block IL-2 signalling on T and B cells. Transplant patients often lack classic infection signs (fever, leukocytosis) — present with altered sensorium, low BP, abnormal LFTs. Low threshold for invasive diagnostics.
sections.Immunosuppression in HSCT patients: Allogeneic HSCT: aggressive myeloablative conditioning + post-engraftment immunosuppression for GVHD → higher infection risk than autologous. Autologous: no GVHD, no post-engraftment immunosuppression, faster reconstitution. Phases: Phase I (<30 days): bacterial neutropenic fever, invasive fungal. Phase II (30–100 days): CMV, adenovirus, BK virus, PCP, Candida, Aspergillus — especially with GVHD. Phase III (>100 days): encapsulated bacteria, VZV, Aspergillus, Toxoplasma. CMV highest risk: seropositive recipient + seronegative donor. Prophylaxis: fluconazole, TMP-SMX (PCP), acyclovir.
sections.Coincident Immunomodulatory Effects of C: Etomidate: inhibits 11β-hydroxylase → adrenal insufficiency even after single dose; decreases NF-kB; recent data do not confirm increased mortality. Sedatives (propofol, midazolam, ketamine): impair phagocytosis, chemotaxis, cytokine release; disturb circadian rhythms. Dexmedetomidine: does NOT impair immunity; improves macrophage function in animal models. Opioids (morphine): NK cell decrease, Th2 shift, decreased cortisol; inadequate human evidence. Catecholamines: primarily β-receptor-mediated anti-inflammatory (decreased TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β; increased IL-10); prolonged exposure may aggravate immunoparalysis; dopamine falling out of favour.
sections.Modulating the Immune System of the Sept: Sepsis-induced immunoparalysis: within 24h of onset, shift to anti-inflammatory state; T-cell exhaustion. Multiple failed trials (antithrombin III, activated protein C, anti-cytokine antibodies) — NOT routinely recommended. MALS: ferritin >4,420 ng/mL (specificity 98%, NPV 97.2%) → anakinra (PROVIDE RCT). Immunoparalysis: HLA-DR <5,000 Ab/cell → rhIFN-γ (PROVIDE RCT). PROVIDE RCT: 42.9% 7-day SOFA improvement in immunotherapy arm vs 10% placebo (p=0.042). Hypogammaglobulinaemia: IgG <500 mg/dL; IgM/IgA-enriched IVIg may benefit; routine IVIg not recommended per SSC 2021.

questions.question: A patient on 40 mg prednisolone daily develops WBC 18×10⁹/L with predominantly mature segmented neutrophils and no left shift. What is the primary mechanism?
options: A: Glucocorticoid-induced inhibition of neutrophil apoptosis, prolonging circulatory lifespan
B: Demargination of neutrophils from the vascular endothelium caused by glucocorticoids
C: Increased bone marrow production of neutrophils stimulated by glucocorticoid-driven myelopoiesis
D: Lymphocyte-to-neutrophil redistribution as glucocorticoids deplete the lymphocyte pool
E: Reactive neutrophilia secondary to cytokine-driven granulopoiesis from concurrent infection
questions.explanation: Glucocorticoids cause neutrophilia by demargination from the vascular endothelium — an acute effect within hours, not increased marrow production. Despite elevated neutrophil counts, GCs impair neutrophil chemotaxis via reduced IL-8, creating a paradox of numerical abundance but functional immunosuppression. GCs simultaneously cause lymphopenia (T-cell apoptosis), not lymphocytosis.
questions.topic: glucocorticoid neutrophilia demargination not marrow IL-8 chemotaxis impaired

questions.question: A rheumatology patient is started on 25 mg prednisolone daily for polymyositis. After how long should Pneumocystis jirovecii chemoprophylaxis be initiated?
options: A: After 30 days of continuous therapy at this dose
B: Immediately at day 1 — any dose above 10 mg requires PCP prophylaxis
C: After 3 months of continuous therapy
D: After serum IgG falls below 500 mg/dL
E: PCP prophylaxis is not indicated unless a calcineurin inhibitor is added
questions.explanation: Patients on ≥20 mg prednisolone equivalent daily for >30 days should receive PCP chemoprophylaxis. This patient is at 25 mg/day (exceeds the ≥20 mg threshold), so prophylaxis is indicated after 30 days. The threshold is specific to both dose AND duration. Tying the trigger to serum IgG instead is incorrect — that is not the basis for glucocorticoid-associated PCP risk.
questions.topic: glucocorticoid PCP prophylaxis 20 mg 30 days threshold prednisolone

questions.question: A 28-year-old post-splenectomy (trauma, 5 years ago) is admitted with rapidly progressive septic shock. Blood cultures grow a gram-positive diplococcus. Which pathogen and prophylactic measure are most likely?
options: A: Streptococcus pneumoniae — vaccination against encapsulated organisms before or after splenectomy
B: Staphylococcus aureus, requiring long-term prophylactic penicillin regardless of vaccination status
C: Enterococcus faecalis — enterococcal vaccination
D: Pseudomonas aeruginosa — fluoroquinolone prophylaxis
E: Candida species — antifungal prophylaxis
questions.explanation: Post-splenectomy patients have lifelong increased risk for encapsulated organisms (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, N. meningitidis) due to loss of splenic IgM memory B-cell opsonisation and macrophage filtration. S. pneumoniae is the most common cause and is a gram-positive diplococcus. Vaccination should be given before elective splenectomy or as soon as possible after emergency splenectomy. This is 'overwhelming post-splenectomy infection' (OPSI).
questions.topic: splenectomy encapsulated S pneumoniae vaccination IgM memory B-cell OPSI lifelong

questions.question: A 55-year-old with rheumatoid arthritis on adalimumab (anti-TNF-α) presents with 3 weeks of cough, night sweats, weight loss, and low-grade fever. CT thorax shows bilateral upper lobe consolidation with cavitation. Which infection is highest on the differential?
options: A: Candida pneumonia from mucosal barrier disruption
B: Invasive aspergillosis from neutrophil deficiency
C: Reactivation tuberculosis
D: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia
E: Community-acquired Streptococcus pneumoniae pneumonia
questions.explanation: Anti-TNF-α agents impair granuloma formation by blocking TNF-α, which is critical for mycobacterial containment. This specifically predisposes to reactivation of latent tuberculosis and other granulomatous infections (Histoplasma, Cryptococcus, Coccidioides). Upper lobe cavitating consolidation with constitutional symptoms is classic for TB reactivation. ALL patients starting anti-TNF therapy must be screened for latent TB. PCP is more linked to high-dose prolonged GC; Aspergillus requires neutropenia.
questions.topic: anti-TNF adalimumab TB reactivation granuloma impaired latent screening upper lobe

questions.question: A patient with metastatic melanoma on pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1) develops severe immune-related pneumonitis requiring high-dose corticosteroids. What is the primary mechanism of subsequent infection risk in this patient?
options: A: Pembrolizumab impairs terminal complement function, predisposing specifically to Neisseria infections
B: PD-1 inhibitors directly cause hypogammaglobulinaemia, mimicking common variable immunodeficiency in these patients over time
C: Immunosuppression results from corticosteroid treatment required for the irAE, not from the checkpoint inhibitor itself
D: Pembrolizumab directly depletes CD4+ T-cells, producing a deficiency analogous to HIV
E: PD-1 blockade directly impairs neutrophil function, increasing gram-negative bacteraemia risk
questions.explanation: Checkpoint inhibitors (PD-1/PD-L1, CTLA-4) have no intrinsic direct immunosuppressive effect — the module terms this the 'Domino effect'. They cause immune-related adverse events (irAEs) such as pneumonitis and colitis. Treatment of irAEs with corticosteroids (and sometimes TNF-α inhibitors) creates iatrogenic immunosuppression, which then risks PCP, IFI, and TB. Attributing direct CD4 depletion, neutrophil impairment, complement blockade, or hypogammaglobulinaemia to the checkpoint inhibitor itself incorrectly borrows mechanisms belonging to other biologic agents (alemtuzumab, eculizumab) rather than how checkpoint inhibitors actually work.
questions.topic: checkpoint inhibitor domino effect irAE corticosteroid immunosuppression no direct mechanism

questions.question: A 35-year-old with multiple sclerosis treated with alemtuzumab (anti-CD52) develops oesophageal candidiasis with CD4 count 85/μL. Which mechanism explains this immune defect?
options: A: Alemtuzumab depletes mature lymphocytes bearing CD52, producing profound T-cell deficiency analogous to HIV/AIDS
B: Myelosuppression from alemtuzumab causes profound neutropenia leading to candidiasis
C: Alemtuzumab blocks the terminal complement pathway specifically, impairing mucosal antifungal defence
D: Alemtuzumab blocks CD52 on granulocytes specifically, impairing neutrophil function and mucocutaneous barrier defence mechanisms
E: Anti-CD52 causes hypogammaglobulinaemia by depleting B-cells, impairing Candida opsonisation
questions.explanation: Alemtuzumab (anti-CD52) depletes mature lymphocytes (T-cells, B-cells, NK cells) by binding CD52, which is expressed on mature lymphocytes. This causes profound CD4+ T-cell depletion analogous to HIV/AIDS, with susceptibility to CMV, HSV, VZV, PCP, tuberculosis, listeriosis, and mucosal candidiasis. The module explicitly states: infections are 'similar to infections like HIV patient.' It is not primarily anti-neutrophil or anti-complement.
questions.topic: alemtuzumab anti-CD52 T-cell depletion HIV-like CMV PCP VZV candidiasis

questions.question: A 44-year-old develops fever and respiratory failure 3 months after allogeneic HSCT. He is on cyclosporine and mycophenolate for GVHD prophylaxis. BAL PCR is positive for Pneumocystis jirovecii. Which infection phase does this represent?
options: A: This infection pattern does not occur after allogeneic HSCT
B: Phase III (late, >100 days) — encapsulated bacteria, VZV, Toxoplasma reactivation
C: Phase I (pre-engraftment, <30 days) — gram-negative bacterial neutropenic infections predominating early
D: Phase II (post-engraftment, 30–100 days) — CMV, PCP, Aspergillus, Candida; especially when GVHD immunosuppression is required for treatment
E: Phase I — attributed specifically to coagulase-negative Staphylococcus and CMV reactivation resulting from total body irradiation conditioning
questions.explanation: At 3 months post-allogeneic HSCT, this is Phase II (post-engraftment, ~30–100 days). GVHD-related immunosuppression and its treatment (cyclosporine, mycophenolate) create vulnerability to CMV, adenovirus, BK virus, PCP, Candida, and Aspergillus. TMP-SMX prophylaxis against PCP is specifically indicated in allogeneic HSCT. Phase I is characterised by bacterial neutropenic fever; Phase III by encapsulated bacteria, VZV, and Toxoplasma.
questions.topic: HSCT phase II post-engraftment PCP CMV Aspergillus GVHD immunosuppression 30-100 days

questions.question: A patient with Guillain-Barré syndrome undergoing plasmapheresis is receiving IV piperacillin-tazobactam for concurrent VAP. Which aspect of plasmapheresis is most relevant to antibiotic management?
options: A: Plasmapheresis removes 90% of serum immunoglobulins, requiring routine IVIg replacement after every session
B: Plasmapheresis can inadvertently remove intravenous antibiotics; re-dosing after the procedure should be considered
C: Plasmapheresis is contraindicated with beta-lactam antibiotics
D: The extracorporeal circuit concentrates antibiotics, increasing nephrotoxicity risk
E: Antibiotic pharmacokinetics are unaffected by plasmapheresis as drugs redistribute rapidly to extravascular compartments
questions.explanation: The module specifically states: 'a provider should be vigilant about inadvertent removal of intravenous antibiotics during these procedures and consider re-dose following completion.' Plasmapheresis removes up to 60% (not 90%) of serum immunoglobulins; IVIg is given when IgG falls below 500 mg/dL — not routinely after every session. The circuit does not concentrate or interact adversely with antibiotics.
questions.topic: plasmapheresis IV antibiotics removal re-dosing 60 percent immunoglobulins IgG 500

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a recognised immune effect of prolonged noradrenaline (norepinephrine) infusion in the ICU?
options: A: Th2-predominant immune shift with prolonged catecholamine exposure
B: Enhanced macrophage phagocytic killing, improving pathogen clearance
C: Potential aggravation of immunoparalysis with prolonged infusion
D: Anti-inflammatory shift via β-receptor stimulation, decreasing TNF-α and IL-6
E: Increased IL-10 production via β-adrenergic receptor pathways
questions.explanation: Catecholamines do not enhance macrophage phagocytic killing — they produce anti-inflammatory effects via β-receptor stimulation (decreased TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β; increased IL-10; Th2 shift). In vitro studies have actually noted increased bacterial growth in the presence of catecholamines. Prolonged exposure may aggravate immunoparalysis, not improve phagocytic function — claiming enhanced phagocytic killing is the one effect not supported by the evidence here.
questions.section: Coincident Immunomodulatory Effects of Commonly Used ICU Drugs
questions.topic: noradrenaline beta receptor anti-inflammatory NOT macrophage phagocytic enhancement immunoparalysis

questions.question: Which immunological adverse effect of etomidate is most specifically documented in this module?
options: A: Etomidate activates cortisol synthesis via glucocorticoid receptor upregulation
B: Etomidate selectively depletes NK cells, impairing innate antiviral immunity
C: Etomidate enhances NF-kB signalling activity, thereby worsening the cytokine storm already present in sepsis
D: Etomidate inhibits 11β-hydroxylase, causing adrenal insufficiency even after a single induction dose
E: Etomidate causes dose-dependent cumulative lymphocyte apoptosis requiring multiple exposures
questions.explanation: Etomidate inhibits 11β-hydroxylase, the enzyme critical for cortisol synthesis, causing adrenal insufficiency even after a single induction dose. This is particularly concerning in septic shock. However, recent systematic reviews have not confirmed increased mortality with etomidate, and adding hydrocortisone at induction did not change outcomes. The module also notes etomidate decreases (not increases) NF-kB signalling.
questions.section: Coincident Immunomodulatory Effects of Commonly Used ICU Drugs
questions.topic: etomidate 11-beta hydroxylase adrenal insufficiency single dose NF-kB decreased mortality uncertain

questions.question: Which ICU sedative has a potentially favourable immunological profile compared to propofol, midazolam, and ketamine?
options: A: Midazolam — least effect on neutrophil chemotaxis among benzodiazepines
B: Dexmedetomidine — does not impair immunity and improves macrophage function in animal models
C: All four agents have equivalent immunological profiles
D: Ketamine — uniquely among ICU sedatives, enhances T-cell proliferation and adaptive immune competence
E: Propofol — reduces cytokine release without impairing macrophage function
questions.explanation: The module states dexmedetomidine 'does not negatively affect immunity, but rather improves macrophage function and has a mortality benefit in animal models.' Propofol, midazolam, and ketamine suppress the innate immune system by impairing phagocytosis, chemotaxis, and cytokine release, and disturb circadian rhythms. While human clinical data are limited, dexmedetomidine has the most favourable preclinical immunological profile among common ICU sedatives.
questions.section: Coincident Immunomodulatory Effects of Commonly Used ICU Drugs
questions.topic: dexmedetomidine macrophage favourable propofol midazolam ketamine suppress innate circadian

questions.question: Which organism is NOT typically associated with bloodstream infections in patients receiving total parenteral nutrition (TPN)?
options: A: Candida species
B: Staphylococcus aureus, a common skin and catheter-related pathogen
C: Streptococcus pneumoniae
D: Acinetobacter species
E: Pseudomonas aeruginosa
questions.explanation: Common TPN-associated bloodstream infections are Staphylococcus, Candida, Acinetobacter, and Pseudomonas — healthcare-associated pathogens exploiting catheter access, impaired NF-kB signalling, and hyperglycaemia. Streptococcus pneumoniae is an encapsulated organism associated with humoral immunity defects (hypogammaglobulinaemia, splenectomy) — NOT the typical TPN catheter-related or metabolic infection pattern.
questions.topic: TPN bloodstream NOT S pneumoniae encapsulated Staphylococcus Candida Acinetobacter Pseudomonas

questions.question: A 62-year-old with pneumonia-related sepsis in the ICU has serum ferritin of 5,200 ng/mL. Based on the PROVIDE RCT, which immune phenotype and targeted intervention should be considered?
options: A: Ferritin >4,420 ng/mL → DIC → administer antithrombin III
B: Ferritin >4,420 ng/mL → T-cell exhaustion → administer polyclonal IVIg
C: Ferritin >4,420 ng/mL → macrophage activation-like syndrome (MALS) → consider anakinra
D: Ferritin >4,420 ng/mL + HLA-DR <5,000 Ab/cell → immunoparalysis → consider rhIFN-γ
E: Ferritin >4,420 ng/mL + HLA-DR >15,000 Ab/cell → hyperinflammatory state → corticosteroids
questions.explanation: In the PROVIDE RCT, ferritin >4,420 ng/mL (specificity 98%, NPV 97.2%) defined MALS. The targeted intervention for MALS was anakinra (IL-1 receptor antagonist). Immunoparalysis is separately defined by HLA-DR <5,000 Ab/cell, with rhIFN-γ as targeted therapy. Combining the ferritin threshold with the immunoparalysis HLA-DR criterion conflates two distinct phenotypes: ferritin alone identifies MALS, not immunoparalysis. Antithrombin III is explicitly listed among the failed treatments not recommended routinely.
questions.section: Modulating the Immune System of the Septic Patient
questions.topic: MALS ferritin 4420 anakinra PROVIDE RCT immunoparalysis HLA-DR 5000 rhIFN-gamma separate

questions.question: A 45-year-old with DLBCL receiving rituximab-based chemotherapy was HBsAg-negative and anti-HBc-positive pre-treatment. Two months later, HBsAg has turned positive with markedly elevated LFTs. What is the mechanism?
options: A: Rituximab-induced direct hepatotoxicity via off-target antibody binding to hepatocyte surface receptors and bile canaliculi
B: De novo HBV infection from blood product transfusion
C: CMV hepatitis reactivation from rituximab-related T-cell suppression
D: Reactivation of latent hepatitis B due to rituximab-induced B-cell depletion and impaired immunoglobulin production
E: Rituximab-induced liver metastases causing obstructive LFT elevation
questions.explanation: Rituximab (anti-CD20) causes profound B-cell depletion and impaired immunoglobulin production, removing humoral control of latent HBV. Anti-HBc-positive with HBsAg-negative indicates past HBV exposure previously contained by humoral immunity — when B-cell function is ablated by rituximab, HBV reactivates. All patients starting rituximab must be screened for HBV (HBsAg and anti-HBc); those with past exposure require antiviral prophylaxis.
questions.topic: rituximab anti-CD20 HBV reactivation B-cell depletion anti-HBc positive screening prophylaxis

questions.question: Which statement best explains the higher infection risk in allogeneic compared to autologous haematopoietic stem cell transplantation?
options: A: The difference is entirely attributable to older recipient age typically seen in allogeneic HSCT populations compared with autologous recipients undergoing transplantation
B: Allogeneic HSCT uses more aggressive myeloablative conditioning AND requires post-engraftment immunosuppression for GVHD prevention
C: Infection risk is equivalent; the only difference is rejection risk
D: Allogeneic HSCT achieves faster engraftment, causing earlier mucosal barrier damage
E: Autologous HSCT requires more intensive post-transplant immunosuppression than allogeneic
questions.explanation: Allogeneic HSCT has higher infection risk for two compounding reasons: (1) more aggressive myeloablative conditioning causing profound and prolonged neutropenia and lymphopenia, and (2) ongoing post-engraftment immunosuppression to prevent GVHD. Autologous HSCT has no GVHD risk, requires no post-engraftment immunosuppression, and has faster immune reconstitution. GVHD itself further impairs immunity, and GVHD treatment intensifies immunosuppression further.
questions.topic: allogeneic autologous HSCT GVHD conditioning infection risk higher immune reconstitution

questions.question: Regarding the immunological effects of glucocorticoids (GCs) in the ICU:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Despite GC-induced neutrophilia, neutrophil chemotaxis is impaired due to reduced production of the chemoattractant IL-8.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Glucocorticoids suppress T-lymphocytes more profoundly than B-lymphocytes, while NK cells are not affected.

statements.label: C
statements.text: GC administration causes lymphocytosis by mobilising lymphocytes from lymphoid organs into the peripheral circulation.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Acute GC administration causes neutrophilia through demargination from the vascular endothelium, not via increased bone marrow production.

questions.explanation: Acute GC-induced neutrophilia through demargination rather than increased marrow production is correct, as is impaired neutrophil chemotaxis from reduced IL-8 despite the neutrophilia, and T-lymphocytes being suppressed more than B-lymphocytes while NK cells remain unaffected. Claiming GCs cause lymphocytosis by mobilising lymphocytes into circulation is false and the key trap here: GCs cause LYMPHOPENIA, not lymphocytosis, through T-cell apoptosis and redistribution out of the circulation.
questions.topic: GC neutrophilia demargination IL-8 chemotaxis impaired T-cell NK unaffected lymphopenia NOT lymphocytosis

questions.question: Regarding the immunological consequences of splenectomy and hyposplenism:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Patients undergoing elective splenectomy should be vaccinated against encapsulated organisms ideally before surgery.

statements.label: B
statements.text: The liver fully compensates for splenic immune filtration within 3 months of splenectomy, restoring normal susceptibility to encapsulated organisms.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Post-splenectomy patients are at lifelong increased risk of infection with encapsulated organisms (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, N. meningitidis) due to loss of splenic IgM memory B-cell opsonisation and macrophage filtration.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Functional hyposplenism (e.g. in sickle cell disease, advanced cirrhosis) confers a similar infection risk profile to post-surgical splenectomy.

questions.explanation: Lifelong increased risk of encapsulated organism infection post-splenectomy, pre-splenectomy vaccination, and functional hyposplenism conferring a similar risk profile to surgical splenectomy are all correct. Claiming the liver fully compensates for splenic filtration within 3 months is false: it does NOT, and post-splenectomy patients retain lifelong increased risk, since the spleen's unique IgM memory B-cell opsonisation of encapsulated bacteria cannot be replicated by hepatic filtration.
questions.topic: splenectomy lifelong encapsulated vaccination IgM B-cell liver no compensation functional hyposplenism

questions.question: Regarding infectious complications of anti-TNF-α therapy:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Anti-TNF monoclonal antibodies cause T-cell depletion analogous to alemtuzumab, producing the same opportunistic infection profile as HIV/AIDS.

statements.label: B
statements.text: All patients commencing anti-TNF therapy should be screened for latent tuberculosis and receive prophylaxis if the screen is positive.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Anti-TNF agents impair granuloma formation, specifically predisposing to reactivation of latent TB, Histoplasma, Cryptococcus, and Coccidioides.

statements.label: D
statements.text: HBV reactivation can occur in patients with past HBV exposure (anti-HBc positive) who receive anti-TNF therapy.

questions.explanation: Anti-TNF agents impairing granuloma formation and predisposing to TB/Histoplasma/Cryptococcus/Coccidioides reactivation, universal latent TB screening before starting therapy, and HBV reactivation risk in anti-HBc-positive patients are all correct. Claiming anti-TNF agents cause T-cell depletion analogous to alemtuzumab is false: they do not deplete T-cells at all — their mechanism is TNF-α blockade impairing granuloma formation, an entirely distinct mechanism from alemtuzumab's direct lymphocyte depletion.
questions.topic: anti-TNF granuloma TB HBV reactivation NOT T-cell depletion alemtuzumab different mechanism

questions.question: Regarding sepsis-induced immunoparalysis:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The initial cytokine storm in sepsis is typically followed within 24 hours by a shift toward an anti-inflammatory state (immunoparalysis), associated with increased susceptibility to nosocomial infections.

statements.label: B
statements.text: HLA-DR expression below 5,000 antibodies/cell on circulating monocytes identifies immunoparalysis in the PROVIDE RCT framework.

statements.label: C
statements.text: In the PROVIDE RCT, ferritin >4,420 ng/mL identified MALS patients who showed improved 7-day SOFA reduction with anakinra versus placebo.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Immunoparalysis predominantly occurs during the initial hyperinflammatory phase and resolves automatically once antibiotics clear the infection.

questions.explanation: The 24-hour shift from cytokine storm to anti-inflammatory immunoparalysis, the HLA-DR <5,000 antibodies/cell threshold for immunoparalysis, and the ferritin >4,420 ng/mL threshold identifying MALS patients who benefited from anakinra in the PROVIDE RCT are all correct. Claiming immunoparalysis occurs during the initial hyperinflammatory phase and resolves automatically with antibiotics is false: it follows the hyperinflammatory phase and does not resolve automatically — it requires sustained immune dysregulation to recover and can persist, contributing to late ICU deaths from secondary infection.
questions.section: Modulating the Immune System of the Septic Patient
questions.topic: immunoparalysis 24h anti-inflammatory HLA-DR 5000 MALS ferritin 4420 PROVIDE anakinra not resolves antibiotics

questions.question: Regarding infection risk after haematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT):

statements.label: A
statements.text: Phase I (pre-engraftment, <30 days) is characterised by bacterial and invasive fungal infections similar to febrile neutropenia after chemotherapy.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Phase II (post-engraftment, 30–100 days) in allogeneic HSCT carries significantly higher risk of CMV, PCP, Candida, and Aspergillus, particularly when GVHD immunosuppression is required.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Allogeneic HSCT recipients carry higher overall infection risk than autologous due to both more aggressive conditioning and ongoing post-engraftment immunosuppression.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Autologous HSCT recipients require post-engraftment immunosuppression to prevent autologous graft-versus-host disease, extending the immunocompromised period.

questions.explanation: Phase I bacterial/fungal infections resembling febrile neutropenia, Phase II's elevated CMV/PCP/Candida/Aspergillus risk (particularly with GVHD immunosuppression), and allogeneic HSCT's higher overall infection risk from both aggressive conditioning and ongoing immunosuppression are all correct. Claiming autologous HSCT requires post-engraftment immunosuppression for a form of GVHD is false: autologous transplants use the patient's own cells, carry no GVHD risk, and require no post-engraftment immunosuppression — a fundamental distinction from allogeneic HSCT.
questions.topic: HSCT phases bacterial fungal CMV PCP allogeneic autologous no GVHD post-engraftment

questions.question: Regarding immune compromise associated with plasmapheresis:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Plasmapheresis can remove up to 60% of serum immunoglobulins and complement per session, transiently impairing humoral immunity.

statements.label: B
statements.text: IVIg replacement is recommended when serum immunoglobulin levels fall below 500 mg/dL following plasmapheresis.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Plasmapheresis has no effect on intravenous antibiotic concentrations; re-dosing post-procedure is therefore never required.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Plasmapheresis catheters act as a breach in the integumentary system, increasing the risk of catheter-associated infections.

questions.explanation: Plasmapheresis removing up to 60% of immunoglobulins and complement, IVIg replacement below the 500 mg/dL threshold, and plasmapheresis catheters breaching the integumentary system are all correct. Claiming plasmapheresis never affects IV antibiotic concentrations and re-dosing is never required is false: the module explicitly warns that antibiotics can be inadvertently removed during the procedure, and re-dosing should be considered afterward.
questions.topic: plasmapheresis immunoglobulins 60 percent IVIg 500 catheter antibiotics removed re-dose

questions.question: Regarding the immunomodulatory effects of commonly used ICU drugs:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Morphine administration shifts the immune response toward a Th1-dominant pattern, increasing resistance to intracellular pathogens.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Propofol, midazolam, and ketamine suppress the innate immune system by impairing phagocytosis, chemotaxis, and cytokine release.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Dexmedetomidine does not negatively affect immunity and improves macrophage function in animal models, suggesting a potentially favourable immunological profile.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Etomidate inhibits 11β-hydroxylase, causing adrenal insufficiency even after a single induction dose.

questions.explanation: Etomidate's inhibition of 11β-hydroxylase causing adrenal insufficiency after even a single dose, the innate-immune-suppressing effects of propofol/midazolam/ketamine, and dexmedetomidine's favourable immunological profile are all correct. Claiming morphine shifts the immune response toward a Th1-dominant pattern is false: it shifts toward a Th2-dominant response instead, reducing Th1-mediated cell-mediated immunity and impairing resistance to intracellular pathogens such as Salmonella, Toxoplasma, and HSV-1 in animal studies.
questions.section: Coincident Immunomodulatory Effects of Commonly Used ICU Drugs
questions.topic: etomidate adrenal propofol midazolam ketamine innate dexmedetomidine macrophage morphine Th2 NOT Th1

questions.question: Regarding immune modulation during pregnancy:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Pregnant women have increased susceptibility to Listeria monocytogenes, Group B Streptococcus, Toxoplasma gondii, and certain viral infections (CMV, varicella, influenza).

statements.label: B
statements.text: The immune modulation of pregnancy is absolute; pregnant women are equally susceptible to all pathogens as non-pregnant women of the same age.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Perinatal viral infections (rubella, CMV, herpes viruses, varicella) can range from asymptomatic infection to congenital syndromes and multi-organ failure depending on timing and maternal immune status.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Pregnancy involves a Th1-to-Th2 shift with suppression of cytotoxic T-cell responses, preventing maternal immune rejection of the fetus bearing paternal antigens.

questions.explanation: The Th1-to-Th2 shift with suppressed cytotoxic T-cell responses protecting the fetus, increased susceptibility to Listeria/GBS/Toxoplasma/viral infections, and the range of perinatal viral infection outcomes from asymptomatic to congenital syndromes are all correct. Claiming pregnancy-induced immune modulation is absolute and confers equal susceptibility to all pathogens is false: the modulation is pathogen-specific, selectively increasing risk from particular organisms rather than uniformly raising susceptibility to everything.
questions.topic: pregnancy Th2 shift Listeria GBS Toxoplasma viral susceptibility not absolute congenital

questions.question: Regarding immune effects of total parenteral nutrition (TPN) in the ICU:

statements.label: A
statements.text: TPN is associated with decreased CD4+ T-cell activity and presence in Peyer's patches, impairing gut-associated adaptive immunity.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Hyperglycaemia from TPN and the requirement for long-term IV access are additional independent risk factors for bloodstream infections.

statements.label: C
statements.text: TPN without concurrent enteral stimulation impairs immune signalling via NF-kB pathways due to absent gut luminal stimulation.

statements.label: D
statements.text: The immunosuppressive effects of TPN are primarily mediated by direct bone marrow cytotoxicity, analogous to the mechanism of cytotoxic chemotherapy.

questions.explanation: TPN's impairment of NF-kB-mediated immune signalling from absent enteral stimulation, reduced CD4+ activity in Peyer's patches, and hyperglycaemia/long-term IV access as additional infection risk factors are all correct. Claiming TPN's immunosuppressive effects are mediated by direct bone marrow cytotoxicity is false: TPN acts through nutritional and metabolic mechanisms, not marrow toxicity — that mechanism instead describes cytotoxic chemotherapy, an entirely different process.
questions.topic: TPN NF-kB CD4 Peyers hyperglycaemia IV access Staphylococcus Candida NOT bone marrow cytotoxicity

questions.question: Regarding the immune effects of rituximab, alemtuzumab, and eculizumab:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Alemtuzumab (anti-CD52) causes T-cell depletion analogous to HIV/AIDS, predisposing to CMV, HSV, VZV, PCP, and tuberculosis.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Checkpoint inhibitors (PD-1 and CTLA-4 blockers) cause immunosuppression indirectly through irAEs requiring corticosteroid or immunosuppressive treatment, not via direct immunosuppressive properties.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Rituximab (anti-CD20) depletes B-cells and impairs immunoglobulin production, leading to HBV reactivation in patients with past hepatitis B exposure (anti-HBc positive).

statements.label: D
statements.text: Eculizumab (anti-C5) increases susceptibility to invasive Aspergillus and Candida infections by blocking the membrane attack complex.

questions.explanation: Rituximab's B-cell depletion causing HBV reactivation risk, alemtuzumab's HIV-like T-cell depletion pattern, and checkpoint inhibitors causing immunosuppression only indirectly through irAE treatment are all correct. Claiming eculizumab increases susceptibility to Aspergillus and Candida is false: by blocking C5 and preventing membrane attack complex formation, eculizumab specifically impairs defence against encapsulated gram-negative diplococci — Neisseria meningitidis and N. gonorrhoeae — not against fungal pathogens, which depend on neutrophil and T-cell immunity respectively.
questions.topic: rituximab HBV alemtuzumab HIV-like T-cell checkpoint domino eculizumab Neisseria NOT Aspergillus

questions.question: A 45-year-old with 60% total body surface area burns develops septic shock 5 days after injury. Which layer of host defence has been most directly compromised by the injury itself, and what proportion of overall host defence does it represent?
options: A: Adaptive immune response, representing approximately 0.1% of overall host defence
B: Complement system specifically, representing approximately 50% of overall host defence
C: Physical barrier, representing approximately 99% of overall host defence
D: Innate immune response, representing approximately 0.9% of overall host defence
E: Humoral immunity specifically, representing approximately 10% of overall host defence
questions.explanation: Physical barriers (skin, cilia, stomach acidity, normal flora) represent the largest proportion (~99%) of overall host defence in this three-layer framework, and major burns are specifically listed as a compromising condition for this layer, alongside trauma, open fractures, and dermatologic conditions. This is distinct from the innate immune response (~0.9% of defence, compromised by conditions such as glucocorticoid use or chemotherapy) and the adaptive immune response (~0.1% of defence, compromised by conditions such as hypogammaglobulinaemia or HIV) — the complement system and humoral immunity are components within these layers rather than possessing independent percentage weightings of their own.
questions.section: How the normal immune function works
questions.topic: three-layers-immune-defence physical-barrier-99-percent burns

questions.question: A patient receives a liver transplant from a donor subsequently found to have significant mannose-binding lectin (MBL) deficiency. The recipient develops severe sepsis from Streptococcus pneumoniae shortly after transplantation. What is the most likely contributing mechanism?
options: A: Donor-derived MBL deficiency impairing opsonisation and complement activation against encapsulated organisms
B: Coincidental community-acquired pneumonia unrelated to the transplant or donor factors
C: Bacterial contamination of the graft during procurement
D: Standard calcineurin inhibitor immunosuppression alone fully explains this presentation, entirely unrelated to any donor MBL status
E: T-cell-mediated graft rejection response predisposing to secondary bacterial infection
questions.explanation: Mannose-binding lectin is an acute phase reactant that binds carbohydrate moieties on pathogens and initiates opsonisation via the complement pathway or direct phagocytosis; adult MBL deficiency is specifically associated with increased risk of severe sepsis from encapsulated organisms such as S. pneumoniae. The module specifically notes that patients receiving a liver transplant from an MBL-deficient donor are at such risk, since the liver is a major source of circulating MBL and donor-derived deficiency can carry over to the recipient. This is a distinct mechanism from graft rejection, is not simply explained away by routine immunosuppression, and the specific donor MBL deficiency detail makes coincidental infection or graft contamination a weaker explanation.
questions.section: How the normal immune function works
questions.topic: MBL-deficiency donor-derived liver-transplant encapsulated-organism-risk

questions.question: Which statement about the Th1/Th2 balance in adaptive cell-mediated immunity is most accurate?
options: A: The Th1/Th2 balance has no established relevance to susceptibility patterns in immunocompromised patients
B: Th2 cells are primarily responsible for defence against intracellular bacterial and viral pathogens
C: Th1 and Th2 responses are activated simultaneously and entirely independently of one another, with absolutely no reciprocal regulation whatsoever occurring
D: Th1 cells mount the dominant response against parasitic infections and allergens
E: Th1 cells generate responses against bacterial and viral pathogens, Th2 cells respond to parasites and allergens, and one subset suppresses the other
questions.explanation: Th1 cells generate responses against common bacterial and viral pathogens, while Th2 cells mount responses against parasites and foreign allergens; the Th1/Th2 balance is maintained by multiple factors, and activation of the Th1 system suppresses Th2 activity and vice versa — a reciprocal, not independent, relationship. Reversing which subset targets which category of pathogen, or claiming the two operate with no interaction, both misstate this relationship, and the Th1/Th2 balance is directly relevant to immunocompromised patients — for example, the Th2-predominant shift seen in pregnancy and with opioid administration elsewhere in this module.
questions.section: How the normal immune function works
questions.topic: Th1-Th2-balance reciprocal-suppression bacterial-viral-vs-parasitic-allergen

questions.question: Regarding the distinction between innate and adaptive immune responses:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The adaptive immune response is highly specific for particular pathogens, distinguishes foreign antigens from host molecules, and generates memory for future exposures.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Pattern recognition receptors (PRRs) on innate immune cells bind pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) and damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), triggering transcription factors such as NF-kB.

statements.label: C
statements.text: The innate immune response is rapidly acting and non-specific, recognising broad pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) via pattern recognition receptors, without generating lasting memory.

statements.label: D
statements.text: The adaptive immune response can mount a fully effective, pathogen-specific defence within minutes of exposure, acting just as rapidly as the innate immune response.

questions.explanation: The innate response being rapid, non-specific, and memory-less is correct, as is the adaptive response being highly specific with generated memory, and PRRs on innate cells binding PAMPs/DAMPs to trigger transcription factors like NF-kB. Claiming the adaptive response acts within minutes, as fast as the innate response, is false: the adaptive response is comparatively slow, requiring antigen presentation, T-cell activation in lymph nodes, and clonal expansion before a pathogen-specific response is mounted — this delay is precisely why the innate response serves as the essential first line of defence.
questions.section: How the normal immune function works
questions.topic: innate-vs-adaptive PRR-PAMP-DAMP adaptive-not-as-fast-as-innate

questions.question: A renal transplant recipient on standard triple immunosuppression develops a markedly elevated tacrolimus trough level. Tacrolimus specifically blocks which step in the T-cell activation pathway?
options: A: The IL-2 receptor (CD25) on the T-cell surface, preventing IL-2 from binding
B: CD52 on the surface of mature T- and B-lymphocytes, tagging them for destruction
C: The mTOR pathway, preventing the downstream effect of IL-2 on T- and B-cell proliferation
D: DNA and RNA synthesis during lymphocyte clonal expansion
E: Calcineurin-mediated dephosphorylation of NFAT, preventing IL-2 gene transcription
questions.explanation: Tacrolimus (along with cyclosporine) is a calcineurin inhibitor: it blocks calcineurin-mediated dephosphorylation of NFAT, preventing NFAT from entering the nucleus and transcribing the IL-2 gene, thereby preventing lymphocyte proliferation at its origin. Blocking the IL-2 receptor directly describes basiliximab and daclizumab; blocking the downstream mTOR-mediated effect of IL-2 (rather than its transcription) describes sirolimus and everolimus; inhibiting DNA/RNA synthesis during clonal expansion describes antiproliferative agents such as mycophenolate and azathioprine; and tagging CD52-bearing lymphocytes for destruction describes alemtuzumab — each a distinct step in the same overall pathway.
questions.topic: tacrolimus calcineurin-inhibitor NFAT IL-2-transcription mechanism-distinction

questions.question: A kidney transplant recipient on routine antimicrobial prophylaxis develops fever and new pulmonary infiltrates 3 months after transplantation. Based on the typical post-transplant infection timeline, which organism is most consistent with this timeframe?
options: A: A surgical site or anastomotic wound infection
B: A routine community-acquired pathogen such as influenza, unrelated to transplant timing
C: Legionella pneumophila, typical of the very late post-transplant period
D: A donor-derived or hospital-acquired drug-resistant gram-negative organism
E: Cytomegalovirus, reflecting the classic opportunistic/reactivation window
questions.explanation: The 1-6 month post-transplant period is characterised by opportunistic infections and reactivation of latent disease, with cytomegalovirus a classically prominent pathogen in this window even with prophylaxis in place, alongside Pneumocystis jirovecii, Nocardia, endemic fungi, and tuberculosis. Donor-derived and hospital-acquired drug-resistant organisms, along with surgical site infections, are typical of the first month; severe community-acquired infections including Legionella typically become more prominent from 6-12 months and beyond, once immunosuppression has often been reduced to maintenance levels — neither timeframe matches this patient's 3-month presentation as well as the opportunistic/reactivation window does.
questions.topic: SOT-infection-timeline 1-6-months CMV opportunistic-reactivation-window

questions.question: Regarding immunosuppressive regimens for solid organ transplantation:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Transplant patients with infection often present with subtle findings such as altered sensorium, hypotension, or abnormal liver function tests, rather than classic fever and leukocytosis.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Standard immunosuppressive protocols typically combine a glucocorticoid, a calcineurin inhibitor, and an antiproliferative agent.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Calcineurin inhibitors and mTOR inhibitors act through an identical molecular mechanism and can be considered fully interchangeable in an immunosuppressive regimen.

statements.label: D
statements.text: mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus) block the downstream effect of IL-2 signalling on T- and B-cell proliferation, rather than preventing IL-2 gene transcription itself.

questions.explanation: The standard triple-therapy combination, mTOR inhibitors acting downstream of IL-2 transcription rather than preventing it, and the often subtle, atypical presentation of infection in transplant recipients are all correct. Claiming calcineurin inhibitors and mTOR inhibitors share an identical mechanism is false: calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine, tacrolimus) prevent IL-2 gene transcription via NFAT, while mTOR inhibitors block the effect of IL-2 once it has already been produced and has bound its receptor — distinct steps in the same pathway, which is precisely why they are often combined rather than substituted for one another.
questions.topic: SOT-triple-therapy mTOR-vs-calcineurin-distinct-steps atypical-presentation

questions.question: A patient with multiple sclerosis treated with natalizumab develops progressive cognitive decline and new focal neurological deficits over several weeks. MRI shows white matter lesions without significant mass effect or contrast enhancement. Which infection is of greatest concern?
options: A: Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy from JC polyomavirus reactivation
B: Cryptococcal meningitis with elevated opening pressure
C: Toxoplasma gondii reactivation, typically producing ring-enhancing lesions with mass effect on imaging
D: A bacterial cerebral abscess requiring urgent surgical drainage
E: HSV encephalitis with temporal lobe involvement
questions.explanation: Natalizumab (targeting the α4β1 integrin) carries a major increased risk of infection, specifically associated with progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) from JC polyomavirus reactivation — a serious, potentially fatal complication with this drug class. The clinical picture of subacute progressive cognitive and focal neurological decline with non-enhancing white matter change on MRI, without mass effect, is classic for PML rather than the ring-enhancing lesions of toxoplasmosis, the meningitic presentation of cryptococcal infection, the temporal lobe predilection of HSV encephalitis, or a discrete, mass-effect-producing bacterial abscess.
questions.topic: natalizumab integrin-blocker PML JC-virus major-infection-risk

questions.question: A 58-year-old with septic shock has serum IgG of 420 mg/dL and IgM of 25 mg/dL. Based on current evidence, what is the most appropriate approach to intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIg) therapy in this patient?
options: A: IVIg has no evidence base in sepsis under any circumstances and should never be considered
B: Routine IVIg should be given to all septic shock patients regardless of immunoglobulin levels, per SSC 2021 guidelines
C: IVIg should be reserved exclusively for patients meeting ferritin-based diagnostic criteria for macrophage activation-like syndrome
D: IVIg is contraindicated in septic shock due to a proven increase in mortality
E: Given the demonstrated hypogammaglobulinaemia, IgM/IgA-enriched IVIg may be considered here, distinct from routine IVIg use
questions.explanation: Standard administration of intravenous immunoglobulin in sepsis is not recommended according to the 2021 Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines for normoglobulinaemic patients, but hypogammaglobulinaemia (IgG <500 mg/dL, or IgM <35 mg/dL, as demonstrated here) has been associated with higher sepsis mortality and has been proposed as a marker identifying a subgroup that may benefit from IgM/IgA-enriched polyclonal IVIg. This is a more nuanced position than either universally recommending or universally prohibiting IVIg in sepsis, and MALS-directed therapy (anakinra, based on ferritin) is a separate immunotherapy pathway from immunoglobulin replacement for hypogammaglobulinaemia.
questions.topic: hypogammaglobulinaemia-sepsis IVIg-enriched SSC-2021-not-routine

questions.question: Regarding transfusion-related immunomodulation (TRIM) following red blood cell transfusion:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Conclusive evidence has definitively established the clinical significance of TRIM in all transfused critically ill patients, settling this as a resolved question.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Leukoreduction (pre-storage leuko-depletion) of RBC units is standard practice at many institutions, reflecting concern about TRIM.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Proposed mechanisms of TRIM extend beyond residual white blood cell contamination to include RBC breakdown products, bioactive lipid accumulation during storage, and extracellular vesicles.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Transfusion-associated immunomodulation has been linked in some studies to increased postoperative infection risk and altered tumour recurrence.

questions.explanation: Leukoreduction as standard practice reflecting TRIM concerns, the expanded range of proposed TRIM mechanisms beyond residual leukocytes, and the associations reported with postoperative infection and tumour recurrence are all correct. Claiming conclusive evidence has definitively settled TRIM's clinical significance is false: the module explicitly states that conclusive evidence for the existence and clinical importance of TRIM is still lacking, and how a critically ill patient with heightened inflammatory and anti-inflammatory factors is affected by TRIM remains an active area of research and discussion.
questions.topic: TRIM leukoreduction mechanisms-beyond-WBC evidence-still-lacking

questions.question: A patient with cytokine release syndrome following CAR-T cell therapy is treated with tocilizumab. What is the mechanism of action of this agent?
options: A: Direct neutralisation of circulating TNF-α
B: Blockade of complement component C5, preventing membrane attack complex formation against encapsulated organisms
C: Binding to CD52 on mature lymphocytes, tagging them for destruction
D: A neutralising monoclonal antibody against the IL-6 receptor, blocking IL-6-mediated signalling
E: Antagonism of the IL-1 receptor, blocking IL-1-mediated signalling
questions.explanation: Tocilizumab is a neutralising anti-interleukin-6 receptor monoclonal antibody, blocking IL-6-mediated signalling; it has been approved for the treatment of inflammatory conditions including cytokine release syndrome, giant cell arteritis, and rheumatoid arthritis. IL-6 is notable among the inflammatory mediators for having both pro- and anti-inflammatory effects. TNF-α neutralisation instead describes agents such as infliximab or adalimumab, IL-1 receptor antagonism describes anakinra, C5 blockade describes eculizumab, and CD52-directed lymphocyte depletion describes alemtuzumab.
questions.section: How the normal immune function works
questions.topic: tocilizumab IL-6-receptor-blockade cytokine-release-syndrome mechanism

questions.question: Regarding the mechanisms of traditional cytotoxic chemotherapeutic agents:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Topoisomerase inhibitors such as etoposide work through the same direct DNA cross-linking mechanism as platinum-based agents.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Antimetabolites such as methotrexate and azathioprine interfere with DNA and RNA synthesis during clonal expansion of immune cells.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Microtubule-damaging agents such as vincristine cause cellular metaphase arrest by disrupting the mitotic spindle.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Alkylating agents such as cyclophosphamide cause cross-linking of DNA strands and abnormal base pairing.

questions.explanation: Alkylating agents causing DNA cross-linking, microtubule-damaging agents causing metaphase arrest via mitotic spindle disruption, and antimetabolites interfering with DNA/RNA synthesis during clonal expansion are all correctly described. Claiming topoisomerase inhibitors share the same cross-linking mechanism as platinum-based agents is false: topoisomerase inhibitors such as etoposide instead cause cellular G2-phase arrest and DNA strand breaks by interfering with topoisomerase enzyme function, a distinct mechanism from the direct DNA cross-linking caused by platinum-based (and alkylating) agents.
questions.topic: chemo-classes alkylating microtubule antimetabolite topoisomerase-distinct-mechanism
